pre_requisites: Basic Electrical Engineering
Engineering Drawing fundamentals
Basics of electrical circuits and components
expected_outcomes: After completion of the course, students will be able to:
Develop electrical schematics using ECAD tools
Perform circuit simulation and analysis
Design basic PCB layouts
Prepare industrial electrical drawings and panel layouts
Apply ECAD tools for electrical and automation applications

topics.title: Introduction to AutoCAD Electrical / ECAD software

pre_requisites: Students should have solid embedded programming skills (microcontroller GPIO, timers,
sensor/actuator interfacing from II-I), understanding of basic control concepts (ON/OFF control,
relay logic, feedback loops), familiarity with industrial sensors and actuators, and ability to read
circuit diagrams and logic diagrams, so this course can focus on PLC ladder logic, HMI design,
and industrial automation protocols.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to design and program PLC ladder logic for industrial automation tasks
(conveyor control, sorting systems, process control), configure basic HMI screens for operator
interface and monitoring, and implement industrial communication protocols (Modbus RTU) to
integrate sensors, actuators, and controllers in a simulated factory automation environment with
proper documentation and safety interlocks.

topics.text: PLC architecture and I/O modules – 4 h
Ladder logic programming basics (contacts, coils, timers, counters) – 8 h
Industrial sensors and actuators integration – 4 h
Safety interlocks and emergency stops – 4 h
Hands-on: Conveyor belt control simulation – 4 h
HMI screen design and navigation – 4 h
Alarm and trend visualization – 4 h
PLC-HMI communication – 4 h
Modbus RTU/TCP protocol – 6 h
Relay logic and control panel wiring basics – 4 h
Mini project: Multi-stage automation system with HMI – 2 h

pre_requisites: Students should have strong automation and control knowledge (PLC programming, industrial
protocols, control loops from II-II), solid understanding of electrical machines and power circuits
(motor types, three-phase systems, inverters/rectifiers from academic courses), foundational
math and simulation skills (MATLAB basics, transfer functions, circuit simulation), and
technical documentation abilities, so this course can focus on EV powertrain components, power
electronics design, and EV system simulation.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to analyze and design EV powertrain components (motors, inverters,
chargers) using power electronics principles, simulate EV drive cycles and power conversion
systems in MATLAB/Simulink with efficiency calculations and thermal considerations, and
explain battery fundamentals and basic BMS architecture including SoC estimation, cell
balancing, and safety mechanisms for lithium-ion battery packs.

topics.text: EV system overview (BEV, HEV, PHEV) – 4 h
Electric motors for EV (BLDC, PMSM, induction) – 6 h
EV performance metrics (range, efficiency, acceleration) – 4 h
DC-DC converters (buck, boost, buck-boost) – 6 h
Inverters and motor drives (VSI, PWM techniques) – 8 h
EV charging systems (AC/DC chargers, fast charging) – 4 h
Power semiconductor devices (MOSFETs, IGBTs) – 2 h
Battery chemistry and characteristics (Li-ion cells) – 4 h
Battery pack design and configuration – 4 h
BMS fundamentals (SoC, SoH, cell balancing basics) – 4 h
Hands-on: EV powertrain simulation in MATLAB/Simulink – 2 h

semesters.semester_title: Advanced BMS, Industrial IoT & Integration Capstone
pre_requisites: Students should have comprehensive EV and power electronics knowledge (motor drives,
inverters, battery fundamentals, basic BMS from III-I), strong embedded and automation
background (microcontroller programming, PLC, industrial protocols from II-I and II-II),
proficiency in simulation and analysis tools (MATLAB/Simulink, circuit analysis), and solid
technical project documentation and presentation skills, so this capstone course can focus on
advanced BMS algorithms, Industrial IoT integration, and end-to-end system design.
expected_outcomes: Students will be able to design and implement advanced BMS algorithms (accurate SoC/SoH
estimation, active/passive balancing, thermal management with fault detection), integrate
Industrial IoT technologies (MQTT, edge computing, cloud dashboards) for real-time battery
monitoring and predictive maintenance, and deliver a complete capstone project integrating BMS
with IIoT connectivity and CAN bus communication that demonstrates industry-ready skills in
EV battery systems, data analytics, and remote monitoring with professional documentation and
live demonstration.

topics.text: Advanced SoC estimation algorithms (Coulomb counting, Kalman filter) – 6 h
SoH estimation and aging models – 4 h
Cell balancing techniques (active vs passive) – 4 h
Thermal management and cooling strategies – 4 h
BMS safety and fault diagnostics – 4 h

modules.module_title: Industrial IoT for Smart Energy Systems

topics.text: IIoT architecture and protocols (MQTT, OPC UA) – 4 h
Edge computing and sensor data acquisition – 4 h
Cloud platforms for battery monitoring (dashboards, analytics) – 4 h
Cybersecurity basics for IIoT – 2 h
CAN protocol and automotive networks – 4 h
Capstone project: IIoT-enabled BMS prototype – 8 h
Project scope: Design a BMS with real-time monitoring via MQTT, CAN bus integration, and cloud dashboard
Students work in teams to integrate hardware (microcontroller, battery simulator), software (BMS algorithms, IoT gateway), and analytics (visualization, alerts)
Deliverables: Working prototype, system architecture diagram, test results, and live demonstration
Technical aptitude (electrical circuits, power electronics, control systems) – 16 h
Domain-specific assessments (EV/BMS/automation case studies) – 16 h
Coding and problem-solving for EEE roles – 16 h
Resume building and LinkedIn optimization – 4 h
Group discussions and technical presentations – 8 h
Mock interviews (technical + HR rounds) – 12 h
